Summary: This study aimed to develop a combined model using radiomics and CT imaging features to differentiate between benign and malignant subcentimeter solid pulmonary nodules (SSPNs). The model, incorporating clinical and radiomics features, demonstrated excellent discriminative ability, potentially aiding radiologists in diagnosing SSPNs.

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual delineation of regions of interest (ROI) of the nodules on CT images, which constitutes segmentation.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by a radiologist, so it is not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts 2107 radiomics features, which are hand-crafted features.

Condition4.reason: The study uses tabular data in the form of radiomic features and clinical/CT characteristics organized for analysis and modeling.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. Instead, it relies on hand-crafted radiomic features and a logistic regression model.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines like the CLEAR checklist.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming to create a representative study population. The criteria are based on nodule size, confirmation of benign or malignant status, and image quality.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is well-defined, with malignant nodules confirmed by pathology and benign nodules confirmed by follow-up or pathology. The follow-up period of more than 2 years for benign nodules is reasonable.

Item4.reason: The study is a single-center retrospective study.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is contrast-enhanced CT, which is a standard imaging technique for evaluating pulmonary nodules. The use of enhanced CT reflects established standardization approaches.

Item6.reason: The study reports the CT acquisition parameters, including tube voltage, tube current, helical pitch, rotation time, and slice thickness, which helps ensure the replicability of the method.

Item7.reason: The median interval time between the CT scans and surgery was 16 days, which is appropriate to validate the presence or absence of target conditions of the radiomics analysis at the moment of the diagnostic imaging exams.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, including manual delineation of the regions of interest (ROI) by a blinded radiologist, avoiding adjacent vessels, bronchi, and normal lung tissue. This provides a transparent description of the segmentation process.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation in this study was performed by a single radiologist.

Item11.reason: The study mentions normalization and resampling during image pre-processing with a fixed bin width of 25, and all images were resampled to [1,1,0] after the B-spline interpolation sampling technology conducted in this study. This indicates appropriate use of image preprocessing techniques with transparent description.

Item12.reason: The study used the Deepwise Multimodal Research Platform version 2.0.1.4, which is based on the mature python Pyradiomics (version 3.0.1) and Scikit-learn (version 0.22) packages. This indicates the use of standardized feature extraction software.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first-order, shape, texture) and feature classes (GLCM, GLSZM, GLRLM, GLDM, NGTDM). It also mentions that all features were named based on the three-level naming method, and each level was concatenated with “_”. This provides transparent reporting of feature extraction parameters.

Item14.reason: The study assessed the interobserver reproducibility of the segmentation by calculating intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) of features, and features with values > 0.75 were included in subsequent analysis. This indicates the removal of non-robust features.

Item15.reason: The study mentions removing less significant features when the linear correlation coefficient between any two features was greater than 0.9. The feature selection method used in this study was the analysis of variance F-test. This indicates the removal of redundant features.

Item16.reason: The study uses a dataset of 324 patients with 2107 radiomic features. The study uses feature selection to reduce the number of features to 4. The number of instances and features in the final training data set is appropriate according to the research question and modeling algorithm.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study mentions that the original dataset was randomly divided into training and validation cohorts, in a ratio of 7:3, and a model was developed using fivefold cross validation. This indicates a proper data partitioning process.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the analysis and handling of potential confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics, such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value. ROC curves were also drawn. This indicates the use of appropriate performance evaluation metrics for the task.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values. This indicates consideration of uncertainty.

Item22.reason: The study does not mention any calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study uses contrast-enhanced CT images. There is no comparison with non-contrast CT images or justification for using contrast-enhanced images.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the combined model with clinical and radiomics models. The DeLong test was used to compare the AUCs of different models. This indicates a comparison with a non-radiomic approach.

Item25.reason: The study does not include a comparison with simple or classical statistical models like a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training and/or validation sets.

Item27.reason: The study does not include external testing with independent data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study states that the datasets are not publicly available due to data sharing not obtaining the informed consent of the patients.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention the availability of code or scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention the availability of the final model in any form.
